answer.rationale: Pan-canonical lexical demonstration. Acts 5:3-4 places lying to the Holy Spirit (pseudomai G5574 with to pneuma to hagion G4151 G40) in direct apposition with lying to God (to theo G2316): Peter says the same act in two clauses, naming the Spirit as God. 2 Cor 3:17 (ho de kyrios to pneuma estin) functionally identifies the kyrios G2962 of the Exodus 34 narrative with the pneuma G4151. Matt 28:19 places the Spirit inside the singular onoma G3686 alongside the Father and the Son in the baptismal formula; one name shared by three referents. 2 Cor 13:14 sets the triadic Pauline benediction with charis G5485, agape G26, koinonia G2842 distributed to Lord Jesus, God (the Father), and the Holy Spirit as one ascription. 1 Cor 2:10-11 says the Spirit searches (ereunao G2045) the depths (bathos G899) of God; only the Spirit of God knows the things of God, since only God can exhaustively know God. Ps 139:7-10 predicates omnipresence (a divine attribute) of YHWH's ruach H7307: nowhere can the psalmist flee from God's Spirit. Heb 9:14 calls the Spirit eternal (pneumatos aioniou G4151 G166). Acts 13:2 has the Spirit speak in the first person (eipen to pneuma to hagion ... aphorisate de moi), commissioning Barnabas and Saul with divine self-reference. 1 Cor 6:19 calls the believer's body a naos G3485 (temple) of the Holy Spirit; the parallel at 1 Cor 3:16 calls the church a naos of God. Ephesians 4:30 says do not grieve (lypeo G3076) the Holy Spirit, a personal verb that excludes an impersonal force. The canon thus predicates of the Spirit divine identity (Acts 5:3-4, 2 Cor 3:17), divine attributes (omniscience 1 Cor 2:10-11, omnipresence Ps 139:7-10, eternity Heb 9:14), divine acts (commissioning Acts 13:2, indwelling 1 Cor 6:19), and inclusion in the singular divine name (Matt 28:19), while preserving personal distinction from the Father and the Son (2 Cor 13:14, John 14:16-17, John 15:26).
answer.notes: The full deity of the Holy Spirit is the second classical Trinitarian boundary, formalized at the First Council of Constantinople in AD 381 against the Pneumatomachi (the Spirit-fighters), also called the Macedonians after Macedonius of Constantinople. The Pneumatomachi accepted the Son's full deity (post-Nicaea AD 325) but denied that the Spirit was likewise homoousios with the Father, treating the Spirit as a creature or as a divine power short of a person. The 381 Council amplified the Niceno-Constantinopolitan Creed's earlier brief clause (and we believe in the Holy Spirit) into the full confession: the Lord, the Giver of life, who proceeds from the Father, who with the Father and the Son together is worshipped and glorified, who spoke through the prophets. Basil of Caesarea's De Spiritu Sancto and Athanasius's Letters to Serapion are the definitive fourth-century defenses. Cult-marker-if-denied is canonically defensible because the canon explicitly equates lying to the Spirit with lying to God (Acts 5:3-4), explicitly identifies the kyrios with the pneuma (2 Cor 3:17), and places the Spirit inside the singular onoma of God (Matt 28:19); denial of the Spirit's full deity therefore breaks the canonical lexical pattern of the one God being Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. Public-record carriers of denial: ancient Pneumatomachi / Macedonians (condemned at Constantinople 381); Jehovah's Witnesses (the Watchtower teaches the Holy Spirit as God's impersonal active force, not a person and not God; rendered in lower case in the New World Translation); Unitarian Universalists (no binding doctrine of God); Christadelphians (Holy Spirit as God's power, not a person); Iglesia ni Cristo (Felix Manalo's Filipino sect; Spirit as God's power, not a divine person); Oneness Pentecostalism (modalism; Spirit as a mode of the one person, not personally distinct, which collapses the Spirit's personhood even while affirming divinity); certain liberal-mainline reductions that treat the Spirit as an aspect of God's activity rather than a divine person. Fruit-vs-precision nuance: most lay believers cannot articulate homoousios for the Spirit, but they pray that the Spirit fill them, they grieve to think of grieving him, and they confess the Spirit's personal divine work; these are functional confessions of the Spirit's deity and personhood. The cult-marker line falls sharply: denial of the Spirit's full deity collapses Trinitarian monotheism and falsifies who God is in his inner life.

stem_audit.notes: The statement 'The Holy Spirit is fully and truly God, co-equal and co-eternal with the Father and the Son' uses creedal phrasing drawn from the Niceno-Constantinopolitan Creed (381) and the Athanasian Creed. However, each clause is a direct lexical gloss of canonical text. 'Fully and truly God' restates Acts 5:3-4 (lying to the Spirit IS lying to God). 'Co-equal' restates Matt 28:19 (one shared name) and 2 Cor 13:14 (triadic benediction). 'Co-eternal' restates Heb 9:14 (pneumatos aioniou, the eternal Spirit). The statement asserts the verdict, but every divine-person and Trinitarian proposition does, and the lexical evidence supports the assertion.

lay_summary.reasoning: Verdict: the Holy Spirit is fully and truly God, the same God as the Father and the Son, not a created being and not just God's energy or influence. The strongest reason from Scripture is the way the Bible directly treats the Spirit as God. In Acts 5:3 to 4, Peter tells Ananias, 'You have lied to the Holy Spirit,' and in the very next breath says, 'You have not lied to men but to God.' Same act, named both ways. In 2 Corinthians 3:17, Paul writes, 'Now the Lord is the Spirit.' In Matthew 28:19, Jesus tells his disciples to baptize 'into the name (singular) of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit,' placing the Spirit inside the one divine name. In 1 Corinthians 2:10 to 11, Paul says the Spirit searches the deep things of God and knows the thoughts of God, because only God can know God exhaustively. Psalm 139:7 to 10 says no one can flee from God's Spirit, anywhere; that is the kind of presence only God has. Hebrews 9:14 calls the Spirit eternal. Acts 13:2 has the Spirit speak in the first person saying, 'Set apart for me Barnabas and Saul,' which is divine self-reference. The strongest counterargument inside the Bible is the set of passages where the Spirit seems to come from the Father or be sent by the Son, suggesting a lower rank. John 15:26 says the Spirit proceeds from the Father. John 14:26 says the Father will send the Spirit in Jesus's name. Critics ask, if the Spirit is sent, how can he be fully God. The historic answer is the same shape as the answer for the Son. The order of sending describes the personal relationships within the one God and the work of redemption, not a difference of nature. The Father sends the Son in the work of redemption; that does not make the Son less than God. The Father and the Son send the Spirit in the work of redemption; that does not make the Spirit less than God. The Bible holds both truths together. There is only one God. The Spirit is fully that one God, alongside the Father and the Son.
lay_summary.denominational_landscape: Agreement on the Holy Spirit's full deity is unanimous across every tracked historic Christian lineage. The Nicene Creed of 325 originally said only 'and in the Holy Spirit,' but the First Council of Constantinople in 381 amplified this into the well-known phrase 'the Lord, the Giver of life, who proceeds from the Father, who with the Father and the Son together is worshipped and glorified, who spoke through the prophets.' This was the formal answer to a fourth-century movement called the Pneumatomachi or Macedonians, who accepted that the Son was fully God but denied that the Spirit was. Basil of Caesarea wrote On the Holy Spirit and Athanasius wrote Letters to Serapion as the definitive defenses. The Catholic Catechism teaches the Spirit as consubstantial with the Father and the Son. Eastern Orthodoxy holds the same creed, with the well-known disagreement about the filioque clause (whether the Spirit proceeds from the Father alone, or from the Father and the Son) being a matter of personal procession, not of the Spirit's deity. Both East and West agree the Spirit is fully God. The Lutheran Augsburg Confession Article I confesses the Holy Ghost as one of the three persons of the same essence and power. The Anglican 39 Articles Article V (Of the Holy Ghost) calls the Spirit 'of one substance, majesty, and glory, with the Father and the Son, very and eternal God.' The Methodist Articles of Religion Article IV uses essentially the same language. The Reformed confessions (Westminster, Belgic, Heidelberg) all affirm the same. The Anabaptist Dordrecht Confession and Pentecostals in the Assemblies of God statement agree. Public-record carriers of denial place themselves outside historic Christianity by their own claim. The Jehovah's Witnesses teach the Holy Spirit as God's impersonal active force, not a divine person, and render the word in lower case in their New World Translation. The Christadelphians teach essentially the same view, calling the Spirit God's power. Iglesia ni Cristo, the Filipino sect, denies the personhood and deity of the Spirit. Unitarian Universalists reject the doctrine of the Trinity altogether. Oneness Pentecostals, while affirming the Spirit's divinity, deny that the Spirit is a distinct person from the Father and Son, which is a different historic Christian boundary. The ancient prototype is the Pneumatomachi, condemned at Constantinople in 381. By every historic Christian standard, these movements teach a different God.

scripture.key_term: ψεύδομαι pseudomai G5574; τὸ πνεῦμα τὸ ἅγιον to pneuma to hagion G4151 G40; θεός theos G2316
scripture.force: Peter v.3: pseusasthai se to pneuma to hagion (why has Satan filled your heart to lie to the Holy Spirit). Peter v.4: ouk epseuso anthropois alla to theo (you have not lied to men but to God). Same act of lying named twice; the Spirit is identified with God by direct apposition in two consecutive sentences. Lexically explicit equation.

scripture.key_term: κύριος kyrios G2962; πνεῦμα pneuma G4151; εἰμί eimi G1510 (present)
scripture.force: ho de kyrios to pneuma estin (now the Lord is the Spirit). Paul is exegeting Exodus 34, where the kyrios is the YHWH of Sinai; he identifies the kyrios of that text with the pneuma. Functional identification of the Spirit within the one divine identity. The verb estin is present indicative, asserting identity not analogy.

scripture.key_term: ὄνομα onoma G3686 (singular accusative); πατήρ pater G3962; υἱός huios G5207; ἅγιον πνεῦμα hagion pneuma G40 G4151
scripture.force: eis to onoma (singular) tou patros kai tou huiou kai tou hagiou pneumatos. Baptize them into THE NAME of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit. One name shared by three referents. Jesus, who has just claimed all authority, places the Spirit alongside the Father in the consecration formula of new disciples into the one God of Israel.

scripture.key_term: χάρις charis G5485; ἀγάπη agape G26; κοινωνία koinonia G2842; κύριος Ἰησοῦς Χριστός G2962 G2424 G5547; θεός theos G2316; ἅγιον πνεῦμα G40 G4151
scripture.force: Pauline triadic benediction: the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ and the love of God and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit be with you all. Three distinct giving relationships, three named persons, one shared divine blessing pronounced over the church. Embeds the Trinitarian shape as devotional habit; the Spirit is set in equal ascription with God the Father and the Lord Jesus.

scripture.key_term: ἐρευνάω ereunao G2045 (searches); βάθος bathos G899 (depths); πνεῦμα pneuma G4151; οἶδα oida G1492 (knows)
scripture.force: The Spirit searches all things, even the depths of God. For who among men knows the things of a man except the spirit of the man within him? In the same way, no one knows the things of God except the Spirit of God. Paul predicates of the Holy Spirit exhaustive knowledge of God's inner life. Only God can know God exhaustively; therefore the Spirit who does so is God.

scripture.key_term: רוּחַ ruach H7307 (Spirit); פָּנֶה panim H6440 (presence); אָנָה anah H575 (where)
scripture.force: Where can I go from your Spirit (meruchecha)? Where can I flee from your presence? If I ascend to heaven, you are there; if I make my bed in Sheol, you are there. Synonymous parallelism equates God's Spirit with God's presence; omnipresence is predicated of the ruach. Omnipresence is a divine attribute that no creature possesses.

scripture.key_term: πνεῦμα αἰώνιον pneuma aionion G4151 G166 (eternal Spirit)
scripture.force: How much more will the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God. The Spirit is called aionios (eternal); eternity is a divine attribute. Christ's self-offering is mediated through the eternal Spirit, placing the Spirit at the heart of the atonement on the divine side.

scripture.key_term: λέγω lego (eipen G2036, aorist); πνεῦμα ἅγιον G4151 G40; ἀφορίζω aphorizo G873 (set apart); ἐγώ ego (moi G1473 dative); ἐμοί emoi
scripture.force: The Holy Spirit said (eipen to pneuma to hagion): Set apart for me (moi, dative first person singular) Barnabas and Saul for the work to which I have called them. The Spirit speaks in the first person, commands, and claims missionaries as belonging to himself. Divine self-reference and personal agency, both exclusive to deity.

scripture.key_term: ναός naos G3485 (temple inner sanctuary); ἅγιον πνεῦμα G40 G4151
scripture.force: Do you not know that your body is a temple (naos) of the Holy Spirit within you, whom you have from God. The Greek naos denotes the inmost sanctuary where God dwells. The parallel at 1 Cor 3:16 calls the church a temple of God (naos theou) in whom the Spirit of God dwells. The same temple language is applied to the Spirit and to God interchangeably.

scripture.key_term: ναός θεοῦ naos theou G3485 G2316; πνεῦμα τοῦ θεοῦ pneuma tou theou G4151 G2316
scripture.force: Do you not know that you are a temple of God (naos theou) and that the Spirit of God (pneuma theou) dwells in you. Names believers as God's temple precisely because God's Spirit dwells in them; the indwelling of the Spirit and the indwelling of God are the same indwelling. Pair with 1 Cor 6:19 (temple of the Holy Spirit).

scripture.key_term: λυπέω lypeo G3076 (grieve); τὸ πνεῦμα τὸ ἅγιον τοῦ θεοῦ G4151 G40 G2316
scripture.force: And do not grieve (lypeite) the Holy Spirit of God, by whom you were sealed for the day of redemption. Grieving is a personal verb; only a person can be grieved. The Spirit is a person with relational affection, capable of being grieved by sin, and is named the Spirit of God.

scripture.key_term: παράκλητος parakletos G3875; ἐκπορεύομαι ekporeuomai G1607 (proceeds); πνεῦμα τῆς ἀληθείας pneuma tes aletheias G4151
scripture.force: When the Helper comes, whom I will send to you from the Father, the Spirit of truth, who proceeds from the Father. The Spirit is the Spirit of truth, sent by the Son from the Father. The procession language (ekporeuomai) places the Spirit's eternal origin within the divine life itself, not as a created sending.

scripture.key_term: ἄλλος allos G243 (another of the same kind); παράκλητος parakletos G3875; πνεῦμα τῆς ἀληθείας pneuma tes aletheias G4151
scripture.force: I will ask the Father, and he will give you another Helper (allos parakletos) to be with you forever, the Spirit of truth. Allos denotes another of the same kind as Jesus the first Helper; if Jesus the parakletos is divine and personal, the other parakletos is divine and personal in the same way. Distinct from yet of the same kind as the Son.

scripture.key_term: πνεῦμα G4151; ἐντυγχάνω entynchano G1793 (intercedes); στεναγμός stenagmos G4726 (groanings)
scripture.force: The Spirit himself intercedes for us with groanings too deep for words. He who searches hearts knows what the mind of the Spirit is, because the Spirit intercedes for the saints according to the will of God. The Spirit has a mind (phronema G5427), intercedes (a personal priestly act), and operates between God and believers from the divine side.

scripture.key_term: ὁδηγέω hodegeo G3594 (guides); πνεῦμα τῆς ἀληθείας G4151; λαλέω laleo G2980 (speaks)
scripture.force: When the Spirit of truth comes, he will guide you into all the truth; for he will not speak on his own authority, but whatever he hears he will speak. Personal verbs guide, hear, speak, declare; pronoun for the Spirit shifts to masculine ekeinos G1565 in v.13-14 despite the neuter gender of pneuma, indicating personal not impersonal reference.

scripture.key_term: πνεῦμα ἅγιον G4151 G40 (named alongside but distinct discussion); πατήρ pater G3962; υἱός huios G5207
scripture.force: Concerning that day or hour no one knows, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father. Sometimes appealed to by those who say only the Father is fully God. The verse does not name the Spirit; it ranks the Son under the Father in respect of the incarnate Son's voluntary kenosis, while the canon elsewhere predicates of the Spirit divine attributes. Complicates the simpler co-equal language but does not deny it once the canon-wide pattern is read whole.

scripture.key_term: רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים ruach elohim H7307 H430 (Spirit of God); רָחַף rachaph H7363 (hovers, broods)
scripture.force: The Spirit of God was hovering over the face of the waters. From the opening of canon, the ruach elohim is on the Creator side of the creation event. The same verb rachaph appears in Deut 32:11 for the eagle hovering over its young; relational personal action. Creator-creature divide places the Spirit on the Creator side.

hermeneutics.notes: Grammatico-historical reading of the canonical lexical pattern is sufficient. No interpretive framework (covenant, dispensational, new-covenant, progressive-covenantal) produces a different verdict on the Spirit's deity; this doctrine sits prior to every framework. Analogia scripturae is heavily invoked because the verdict rests on the whole canon (Acts 5:3-4, 2 Cor 3:17, Matt 28:19, 2 Cor 13:14, 1 Cor 2:10-11, Ps 139:7-10, Heb 9:14, Acts 13:2, 1 Cor 6:19 with 3:16, Eph 4:30, Gen 1:2) rather than on any single passage. Progressive revelation is a factor: the Old Testament shows the ruach of God as the creating, sustaining, prophesying, and indwelling presence of God; the New Testament discloses that this Spirit is personally distinct from the Father and the Son while sharing the one divine essence. The OT does not retroactively teach the developed Trinitarian doctrine, but it constrains it: monotheism is non-negotiable, and the New Testament's predication of divine attributes and self-reference to the Spirit demands inclusion within the one God of Israel. Competing-lens verdicts: none within historic Christianity. Pneumatomachi / Macedonian / JW readings are not a different valid framework; they are exegetically judged wrong by the canon-wide lexical pattern (Acts 5:3-4 alone refutes the impersonal-force reading).

counter_witness.anchor: Niceno-Constantinopolitan Creed (381), amplifying the Nicene clause on the Holy Spirit; CCEL Schaff Creeds of Christendom Vol II
counter_witness.key_phrase: And in the Holy Spirit, the Lord, the Giver of life, who proceeds from the Father, who with the Father and the Son together is worshipped and glorified, who spoke through the prophets.

counter_witness.anchor: Athanasian Creed (Quicunque Vult), CCEL Schaff Creeds of Christendom Vol II
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Father eternal, the Son eternal, and the Holy Ghost eternal; and yet they are not three eternals, but one eternal. So likewise the Father is God, the Son is God, and the Holy Ghost is God.

counter_witness.anchor: Basil of Caesarea, De Spiritu Sancto (On the Holy Spirit), c. AD 375; CCEL NPNF Series 2 Vol 8
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Spirit is co-numbered with the Father and the Son in the baptismal formula and the doxology, and therefore is to be worshipped and glorified together with them as fully divine, not as creature.

counter_witness.anchor: Athanasius, Letters to Serapion on the Holy Spirit, c. AD 359; CCEL NPNF Series 2 Vol 4
counter_witness.key_phrase: If the Spirit were a creature, he could not be ranked with the Father and the Son in the indivisible Triad; but Scripture so ranks him, therefore he is of the same essence.

counter_witness.anchor: Catechism of the Catholic Church, paragraphs 243-248, 253 (the dogma of the Holy Trinity, the Holy Spirit consubstantial)
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Holy Spirit is consubstantial with the Father and the Son. With them he is inseparably the one God, adored and glorified together with the Father and the Son.

counter_witness.anchor: Augsburg Confession (1530), Article I, Of God; bookofconcord.org
counter_witness.key_phrase: There is one Divine Essence; yet there are three Persons, of the same essence and power, who also are coeternal, the Father, the Son, and the Holy Ghost.

counter_witness.anchor: Thirty-Nine Articles of Religion (1571), Article V, Of the Holy Ghost
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Holy Ghost, proceeding from the Father and the Son, is of one substance, majesty, and glory with the Father and the Son, very and eternal God.

counter_witness.anchor: Westminster Confession of Faith (1646), Chapter 2, paragraph 3
counter_witness.key_phrase: In the unity of the Godhead there be three Persons of one substance, power, and eternity: God the Father, God the Son, and God the Holy Ghost. The Holy Ghost eternally proceeding from the Father and the Son.

counter_witness.anchor: Methodist Articles of Religion (Wesley's abridgement of the 39 Articles), Article IV, Of the Holy Ghost
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Holy Ghost, proceeding from the Father and the Son, is of one substance, majesty, and glory with the Father and the Son, very and eternal God.

counter_witness.anchor: Dordrecht Confession of Faith (1632), Article I, Of God and the Creation of All Things
counter_witness.key_phrase: We believe in one eternal, almighty, and incomprehensible God, Father, Son, and Holy Ghost; in three persons or being, coequal in power, glory, majesty, and dominion.

counter_witness.anchor: Assemblies of God, Statement of Fundamental Truths, Article 2 (The One True God / Adorable Godhead)
counter_witness.key_phrase: The one true God has revealed Himself as Father, Son, and Holy Ghost, three persons in the one Godhead, eternally self-existent and coequal.

counter_witness.anchor: Orthodox Church in America, Doctrine: The Nicene Creed and the Holy Spirit
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Holy Spirit is the Lord, the Giver of Life, who proceeds from the Father, who is worshipped and glorified together with the Father and the Son. East and West agree on the Spirit's full deity.

web.quote: Acts 5:3: pseusasthai se to pneuma to hagion (to lie to the Holy Spirit). Verb pseudomai G5574 with the Holy Spirit as the addressee of the lie.
Acts 5:4: ouk epseuso anthropois alla to theo (you have not lied to men but to God). Same verb, now with to theo G2316 as the addressee. The Spirit is named God.
2 Cor 3:17: ho de kyrios to pneuma estin (now the Lord is the Spirit). Present indicative of eimi; identity not analogy. Kyrios G2962 = pneuma G4151 in Paul's Exodus 34 exegesis.
Matt 28:19: eis to onoma (singular accusative G3686) tou patros kai tou huiou kai tou hagiou pneumatos. One name, three referents including the Holy Spirit.
2 Cor 13:14: charis G5485 tou kyriou Iesou Christou, agape G26 tou theou, koinonia G2842 tou hagiou pneumatos. Triadic benediction; Spirit set in equal ascription.
1 Cor 2:10-11: to pneuma panta ereuna (G2045), kai ta bathe (G899) tou theou. The Spirit searches even the depths of God; only God exhaustively knows God.
Ps 139:7: anah elek meruchecha (H7307); where can I flee from your Spirit. Omnipresence predicated of God's ruach; a divine attribute.
Heb 9:14: dia pneumatos aioniou (G4151 G166). The Spirit is named eternal; eternity is a divine attribute.
Acts 13:2: eipen to pneuma to hagion aphorisate de moi (set apart for me, dative first-person singular). The Spirit speaks and self-references with moi G1473.
1 Cor 6:19: to soma hymon naos G3485 tou en hymin hagiou pneumatos. Temple of the Holy Spirit. Pair with 1 Cor 3:16 (naos theou).
Eph 4:30: me lypeite (G3076) to pneuma to hagion tou theou. Grieving is a personal verb; the Spirit is a person, not an impersonal force.
G4151 pneuma: when modified by hagion, references the Holy Spirit; carries personal agency (speaks, teaches, grieves) and divine attributes (eternal, omnipresent).
Augsburg I: three Persons of the same essence and power, coeternal, the Father, the Son, and the Holy Ghost.
Methodist Article IV: The Holy Ghost, proceeding from the Father and the Son, is of one substance, majesty, and glory with the Father and the Son, very and eternal God.
Athanasian Creed: Father eternal, Son eternal, Holy Ghost eternal; yet not three eternals but one eternal. The Father God, the Son God, the Holy Ghost God.
